text.NRSV: Paul, called to be an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God, and our brother Sosthenes,
text.Rotherham: Paul, a called apostle of Jesus Christ, through God’s will,—and Sosthenes the brother,—

text.NRSV: To the church of God that is in Corinth, to those who are sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints, together with all those who in every place call on the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, both their Lord and ours:
text.Rotherham: Unto the assembly of God which is in Corinth, sanctified in Christ Jesus, called saints,—with all who call upon the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, in every place,—their Lord and ours:

text.NRSV: Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
text.Rotherham: Favour unto you, and peace, from God our Father, and Lord Jesus Christ.

text.NRSV: I give thanks to my God always for you because of the grace of God that has been given you in Christ Jesus,
text.Rotherham: I give thanks unto my God, at all times, concerning you, by reason of the favour of God given unto you in Christ Jesus,—

text.NRSV: for in every way you have been enriched in him, in speech and knowledge of every kind—
text.Rotherham: That, in everything, ye have been enriched in him—in all discourse and in all knowledge;

text.NRSV: just as the testimony of Christ has been strengthened among you—
text.Rotherham: Even as, the witness of the Christ, hath been confirmed in you,

text.NRSV: so that you are not lacking in any spiritual gift as you wait for the revealing of our Lord Jesus Christ.
text.Rotherham: So that ye come short in no gift of favour,—ardently awaiting the revelation of our Lord Jesus Christ:

text.NRSV: He will also strengthen you to the end, so that you may be blameless on the day of our Lord Jesus Christ.
text.Rotherham: Who will also confirm you unto the end, unaccusable in the day of our Lord Jesus [Christ]:

text.NRSV: God is faithful; by him you were called into the fellowship of his Son, Jesus Christ our Lord.
text.Rotherham: Faithful, is God, through whom ye have been called into the fellowship of his Son Jesus Christ our Lord.

text.NRSV: Now I appeal to you, brothers and sisters, by the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that all of you be in agreement and that there be no divisions among you, but that you be united in the same mind and the same purpose.
text.Rotherham: But I beseech you, brethren, through the name of our Lord Jesus Christ,—that, the same thing, ye, all, be saying, and that there be not, among you, divisions; but that ye be fitly joined together—in the same mind, and in the same judgment.

text.NRSV: For it has been reported to me by Chloe’s people that there are quarrels among you, my brothers and sisters.
text.Rotherham: For it hath been signified unto me, concerning you, my brethren,—by them who are of [the household of] Chloe,—that there are strifes among you,

text.NRSV: What I mean is that each of you says, “I belong to Paul,” or “I belong to Apollos,” or “I belong to Cephas,” or “I belong to Christ.”
text.Rotherham: Now I mean this,—that, each one of you, is saying—I, indeed, am of Paul, but, I, of Apollos, but, I, of Cephas, but, I, of Christ:

text.NRSV: Has Christ been divided? Was Paul crucified for you? Or were you baptized in the name of Paul?
text.Rotherham: The Christ is divided! Was, Paul, crucified for you? Or, into the name of Paul, were ye immersed?

text.NRSV: I thank God that I baptized none of you except Crispus and Gaius,
text.Rotherham: I give thanks, that, none of you, did I immerse—save Crispus and Gaius,

text.NRSV: so that no one can say that you were baptized in my name.
text.Rotherham: Lest any should say—into my own name, I immersed:

text.NRSV: (I did baptize also the household of Stephanas; beyond that, I do not know whether I baptized anyone else.)
text.Rotherham: Yea! I immersed the house of Stephanas also,—besides, I know not whether, anyone else, I immersed.

text.NRSV: For Christ did not send me to baptize but to proclaim the gospel, and not with eloquent wisdom, so that the cross of Christ might not be emptied of its power.
text.Rotherham: For Christ sent me not, to be immersing, but to be telling the good news,—not with wisdom of discourse, lest, void should be made, the cross of the Christ.

text.NRSV: For the message about the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God.
text.Rotherham: For, the discourse which concerneth the cross, unto them, indeed, who are perishing, is, foolishness; but, unto them who are being saved—unto us, it is, God’s power.

text.NRSV: For it is written, “I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and the discernment of the discerning I will thwart.”
text.Rotherham: For it is written—I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and, the discernment of the discerning, will I set aside.

text.NRSV: Where is the one who is wise? Where is the scribe? Where is the debater of this age? Has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?
text.Rotherham: Where is the wise? Where is the scribe? Where is the disputer of this age? Hath not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?

text.NRSV: For since, in the wisdom of God, the world did not know God through wisdom, God decided, through the foolishness of our proclamation, to save those who believe.
text.Rotherham: For, seeing that, in the wisdom of God, the world, through its wisdom, did not get to knew God, God was well-pleased—through the foolishness of the thing proclaimed, to save them that believe.

text.NRSV: For Jews demand signs and Greeks desire wisdom,
text.Rotherham: Seeing that both, Jews for signs, do ask, and, Greeks for wisdom, do seek,

text.NRSV: but we proclaim Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles,
text.Rotherham: Whereas, we, proclaim a Christ who hath been crucified,—unto Jews, indeed, an occasion of stumbling, and, unto Gentiles, foolishness;

text.NRSV: but to those who are the called, both Jews and Greeks, Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God.
text.Rotherham: But, unto the called themselves—both Jews and Greeks, Christ, God’s power, and, God’s wisdom.

text.NRSV: For God’s foolishness is wiser than human wisdom, and God’s weakness is stronger than human strength.
text.Rotherham: Because, God’s foolish thing, is, wiser than men, and, God’s weak thing, mightier than men.

text.NRSV: Consider your own call, brothers and sisters: not many of you were wise by human standards, not many were powerful, not many were of noble birth.
text.Rotherham: For be looking at the calling of you, brethren,—that [there were] not many wise, according to flesh. Not many powerful, not many high-born:

text.NRSV: But God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong;
text.Rotherham: On the contrary—the foolish things of the world, hath God chosen, that he might put to shame them who are wise, and, the weak things of the world, hath God chosen, that he might put to shame the things that are mighty,

text.NRSV: God chose what is low and despised in the world, things that are not, to reduce to nothing things that are,
text.Rotherham: And, the low-born things of the world, and the things that are despised, hath God chosen,—[and] the things that are not,—that, the things that are, he might bring to nought;

text.NRSV: so that no one might boast in the presence of God.
text.Rotherham: So that no flesh should boast before God.

text.NRSV: He is the source of your life in Christ Jesus, who became for us wisdom from God, and righteousness and sanctification and redemption,
text.Rotherham: But, of him, are, ye, in Christ Jesus, who hath been made wisdom unto us, from God,—both righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption:

text.NRSV: in order that, as it is written, “Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord.”
text.Rotherham: In order that, even as it is written—He that boasteth, in the Lord, let him boast.

text.NRSV: When I came to you, brothers and sisters, I did not come proclaiming the mystery of God to you in lofty words or wisdom.
text.Rotherham: I, therefore, brethren, when I came unto you, came, not with excellency of discourse or wisdom, declaring unto you the mystery of God;

text.NRSV: For I decided to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ, and him crucified.
text.Rotherham: For I had not determined to know anything among you, save Jesus Christ,—and, him, as one who had been crucified!

text.NRSV: And I came to you in weakness and in fear and in much trembling.
text.Rotherham: I, therefore in weakness, and in fear, and in much trembling, came to be with you,

text.NRSV: My speech and my proclamation were not with plausible words of wisdom, but with a demonstration of the Spirit and of power,
text.Rotherham: And, my discourse, and what I proclaimed, were not in suasive words of wisdom, but in demonstration of Spirit and power:

text.NRSV: so that your faith might rest not on human wisdom but on the power of God.
text.Rotherham: In order that, your faith, might not be in men’s wisdom, but in God’s power.

text.NRSV: Yet among the mature we do speak wisdom, though it is not a wisdom of this age or of the rulers of this age, who are doomed to perish.
text.Rotherham: Wisdom, however, we do speak, among the full-grown,—wisdom, indeed, not of this age, nor of the rulers of this age, who are to come to nought;

text.NRSV: But we speak God’s wisdom, secret and hidden, which God decreed before the ages for our glory.
text.Rotherham: But we speak, God’s, wisdom, in a sacred secret, that hidden [wisdom], which God marked out beforehand, before the ages, for our glory,—

text.NRSV: None of the rulers of this age understood this; for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.
text.Rotherham: Which, none of the rulers of this age had come to know, for, had they known, not, in that case, the Lord of the glory, would they have crucified!

text.NRSV: But, as it is written, “What no eye has seen, nor ear heard, nor the human heart conceived, what God has prepared for those who love him”—
text.Rotherham: But, even as it is written—The things which eye hath not seen, and ear hath not heard, and upon the heart of man have not come up,—whatsoever things God hath prepared for them that love him,

text.NRSV: these things God has revealed to us through the Spirit; for the Spirit searches everything, even the depths of God.
text.Rotherham: Unto us, in fact, hath God revealed through the Spirit; for, the Spirit, into all things, maketh search, yea! the deep things of God.

text.NRSV: For what human being knows what is truly human except the human spirit that is within? So also no one comprehends what is truly God’s except the Spirit of God.
text.Rotherham: For who of men knoweth the things of a man,—save the spirit of the man that is in him? thus, even the things of God, hath no one come to know, save the Spirit of God.

text.NRSV: Now we have received not the spirit of the world, but the Spirit that is from God, so that we may understand the gifts bestowed on us by God.
text.Rotherham: But, as for us,—not the spirit of the world, have we received, but the Spirit which is of God,—that we might know the things which, by God, have been given in favour unto us:—

text.NRSV: And we speak of these things in words not taught by human wisdom but taught by the Spirit, interpreting spiritual things to those who are spiritual.
text.Rotherham: Which we also speak—not in words taught of human wisdom, but in such as are taught of [the] Spirit, by spiritual words, spiritual things, explaining.

text.NRSV: Those who are unspiritual do not receive the gifts of God’s Spirit, for they are foolishness to them, and they are unable to understand them because they are spiritually discerned.
text.Rotherham: But, a man of the soul, doth not welcome the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness unto him, and he cannot get to know them, because, spiritually, are they examined;

text.NRSV: Those who are spiritual discern all things, and they are themselves subject to no one else’s discernment.
text.Rotherham: But, the man of the spirit, on the one hand, examineth all things, but, on the other, he himself, by no one, is examined.

text.NRSV: “For who has known the mind of the Lord so as to instruct him?” But we have the mind of Christ.
text.Rotherham: For who hath come to know the mind of the Lord, that shall instruct him? But, we, have, the mind of Christ.

text.NRSV: And so, brothers and sisters, I could not speak to you as spiritual people, but rather as people of the flesh, as infants in Christ.
text.Rotherham: I, therefore, brethren, have not been able to speak unto you, as unto men of the Spirit, but as unto men of the flesh—as unto babes in Christ:—

text.NRSV: I fed you with milk, not solid food, for you were not ready for solid food. Even now you are still not ready,
text.Rotherham: With milk, have I fed you, not, with meat; for, not yet, have ye been able;—nay! not [yet] even now, are ye able,

text.NRSV: for you are still of the flesh. For as long as there is jealousy and quarreling among you, are you not of the flesh, and behaving according to human inclinations?
text.Rotherham: For ye are yet fleshly. For, whereas there are, among you, jealousy and strife, are ye not, fleshly, and, after the manner of men, walking?

text.NRSV: For when one says, “I belong to Paul,” and another, “I belong to Apollos,” are you not merely human?
text.Rotherham: For, as soon as one beginneth to say—I, indeed, am of Paul! and another—I, of Apollos! are ye not, men?

text.NRSV: What then is Apollos? What is Paul? Servants through whom you came to believe, as the Lord assigned to each.
text.Rotherham: What then is Apollos? and, what is Paul? ministers through whom ye believed, and, each, as the Lord, gave to him.

text.NRSV: I planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the growth.
text.Rotherham: I, planted, Apollos, watered,—but, God, caused to, grow.

text.NRSV: So neither the one who plants nor the one who waters is anything, but only God who gives the growth.
text.Rotherham: So that, [neither] is he that planteth anything, nor, he that watereth,—but, God, who causeth to, grow.

text.NRSV: The one who plants and the one who waters have a common purpose, and each will receive wages according to the labor of each.
text.Rotherham: Moreover, he that planteth and he that watereth, are one:—howbeit, each one, his own reward, shall receive,—according to his own labour.

text.NRSV: For we are God’s servants, working together; you are God’s field, God’s building.
text.Rotherham: For we are, God’s, fellow-workmen: ye are, God’s, farm, God’s building.

text.NRSV: According to the grace of God given to me, like a skilled master builder I laid a foundation, and someone else is building on it. Each builder must choose with care how to build on it.
text.Rotherham: According to the favour of God which hath been given unto me, as a wise master-builder, I laid a foundation, whereas, another, is building thereon; but, let each one, see, how he buildeth thereon;

text.NRSV: For no one can lay any foundation other than the one that has been laid; that foundation is Jesus Christ.
text.Rotherham: For, other foundation, can, no one, lay, than that which is lying, which is, Jesus Christ.

text.NRSV: Now if anyone builds on the foundation with gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, straw—
text.Rotherham: But, if anyone is building, upon the foundation—gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, straw,

text.NRSV: the work of each builder will become visible, for the Day will disclose it, because it will be revealed with fire, and the fire will test what sort of work each has done.
text.Rotherham: Each one’s work, shall be made, manifest; for, the day, will make it plain, because, by fire, is it to be revealed,—and, each one’s work, of what sort it is, the fire itself will prove:—

text.NRSV: If what has been built on the foundation survives, the builder will receive a reward.
text.Rotherham: If, anyone’s work, shall abide, which he built, a reward, shall he receive,—

text.NRSV: If the work is burned up, the builder will suffer loss; the builder will be saved, but only as through fire.
text.Rotherham: If, anyone’s work, shall be burnt up, he shall suffer loss, but shall, himself, be saved—though, thus, as through fire.

text.NRSV: Do you not know that you are God’s temple and that God’s Spirit dwells in you?
text.Rotherham: Know ye not that ye are a shrine of God, and that the Spirit of God within you doth dwell?

text.NRSV: If anyone destroys God’s temple, God will destroy that person. For God’s temple is holy, and you are that temple.
text.Rotherham: If anyone doth mar the shrine of God, God, will mar him; for the shrine of God is holy,—and such are ye.

text.NRSV: Do not deceive yourselves. If you think that you are wise in this age, you should become fools so that you may become wise.
text.Rotherham: Let no one be deceiving himself:—if anyone imagineth himself to be wise among you, in this age, let him become foolish, that he may become wise;

text.NRSV: For the wisdom of this world is foolishness with God. For it is written, “He catches the wise in their craftiness,”
text.Rotherham: For, the wisdom of this world, is, foolishness with God; for it is written—He that taketh the wise in their knavery,—

text.NRSV: and again, “The Lord knows the thoughts of the wise, that they are futile.”
text.Rotherham: And again—The Lord taketh note of the speculations of the wise, that they are vain.

text.NRSV: So let no one boast about human leaders. For all things are yours,
text.Rotherham: So then, let, no one, be boasting in men; for, all things, are yours—

text.NRSV: whether Paul or Apollos or Cephas or the world or life or death or the present or the future—all belong to you,
text.Rotherham: Whether Paul, or Apollos, or Cephas, or the world, or life, or death, or things present, or things to come, all, are, yours,

text.NRSV: and you belong to Christ, and Christ belongs to God.
text.Rotherham: And, ye, are Christ’s,—and, Christ, is, God’s.

text.NRSV: Think of us in this way, as servants of Christ and stewards of God’s mysteries.
text.Rotherham: Let a man, so, be reckoning of us, as officers of Christ, and stewards of sacred secrets of God.

text.NRSV: Moreover, it is required of stewards that they be found trustworthy.
text.Rotherham: Here, furthermore, it is sought in stewards, that, faithful, one be found.

text.NRSV: But with me it is a very small thing that I should be judged by you or by any human court. I do not even judge myself.
text.Rotherham: With me, however, it counteth for the very smallest thing, that, by you, I should be examined, or by a human day. Nay! I am not even examining myself,

text.NRSV: I am not aware of anything against myself, but I am not thereby acquitted. It is the Lord who judges me.
text.Rotherham: For, of nothing, to myself, am I conscious: nevertheless, not hereby, am I declared righteous, but, he that doth examine me, is the Lord.

text.NRSV: Therefore do not pronounce judgment before the time, before the Lord comes, who will bring to light the things now hidden in darkness and will disclose the purposes of the heart. Then each one will receive commendation from God.
text.Rotherham: So then, not before the fitting time, be judging anything,—until the Lord shall come, who will both bring to light the hidden things of darkness, and make manifest the counsels of the hearts; and, then, the praise shall come to each one, from God.

text.NRSV: I have applied all this to myself and Apollos for your benefit, brothers and sisters, so that you may learn through us the meaning of the saying, “Nothing beyond what is written,” so that none of you will be puffed up in favor of one against another.
text.Rotherham: But, these things, brethren, have I transferred unto myself and Apollos, for your sakes, that, in us, ye might learn [the lesson] —Not beyond the things that are written…! that ye do not puff yourselves up, individually, for this one against that.

text.NRSV: For who concedes you any superiority? What do you have that you did not receive? And if you received it, why do you boast as if it were not a gift?
text.Rotherham: For who maketh thee to differ? and what hast thou which thou didst not receive? But, if thou didst even receive it, why dost thou boast, as though thou hadst not received it?

text.NRSV: Already you have all you want! Already you have become rich! Quite apart from us you have become kings! Indeed, I wish that you had become kings, so that we might be kings with you!
text.Rotherham: Already, ye have become full, already, ye are become rich,—apart from us, ye are become kings! And I would indeed ye had become kings, that, we also, with you, might have together become kings!

text.NRSV: For I think that God has exhibited us apostles as last of all, as though sentenced to death, because we have become a spectacle to the world, to angels and to mortals.
text.Rotherham: For I think that, God, hath set forth, us the apostles, to be last of all, as men devoted to death,—in that, a spectacle, have we been made, unto the world,—both unto messengers and unto men.

text.NRSV: We are fools for the sake of Christ, but you are wise in Christ. We are weak, but you are strong. You are held in honor, but we in disrepute.
text.Rotherham: We, are foolish for Christ’s sake, but, ye, prudent in Christ; we are weak, but, ye, mighty; ye, all-glorious, but, we, dishonoured.

text.NRSV: To the present hour we are hungry and thirsty, we are poorly clothed and beaten and homeless,
text.Rotherham: Until the present hour, we both hunger and thirst, and are naked, and are buffeted, and are wanderers,

text.NRSV: and we grow weary from the work of our own hands. When reviled, we bless; when persecuted, we endure;
text.Rotherham: And toil, working with our own hands: being reviled, we bless, being persecuted, we hold on,

text.NRSV: when slandered, we speak kindly. We have become like the rubbish of the world, the dregs of all things, to this very day.
text.Rotherham: Being defamed, we beseech: as the sweepings of the world have we become, the offscouring of all—until even now.

text.NRSV: I am not writing this to make you ashamed, but to admonish you as my beloved children.
text.Rotherham: Not, to shame you, write I these things; but, as my beloved children, I admonish you.

text.NRSV: For though you might have ten thousand guardians in Christ, you do not have many fathers. Indeed, in Christ Jesus I became your father through the gospel.
text.Rotherham: For, though myriads of tutors ye should have in Christ, yet not many fathers; for, in Christ Jesus, through means of the joyful message, I, begat you.

text.NRSV: I appeal to you, then, be imitators of me.
text.Rotherham: I beseech you, therefore,—become imitators of me.

text.NRSV: For this reason I sent you Timothy, who is my beloved and faithful child in the Lord, to remind you of my ways in Christ Jesus, as I teach them everywhere in every church.
text.Rotherham: For this cause, sent I unto you, Timothy,—who is my beloved and faithful child in the Lord, who shall put, you, in mind of my ways which are in Christ [Jesus], even as, everywhere, in every assembly, I teach.

text.NRSV: But some of you, thinking that I am not coming to you, have become arrogant.
text.Rotherham: But, as though I were not coming unto you, some are puffed up!

text.NRSV: But I will come to you soon, if the Lord wills, and I will find out not the talk of these arrogant people but their power.
text.Rotherham: Howbeit, I will come quickly unto you,—if, the Lord, please,—and will get to know—not the speech of them who are puffed up, but, the power;

text.NRSV: For the kingdom of God depends not on talk but on power.
text.Rotherham: For, not in speech, is the kingdom of God, but, in power.

text.NRSV: What would you prefer? Am I to come to you with a stick, or with love in a spirit of gentleness?
text.Rotherham: What will ye? that, with a rod, I should come unto you? or, with love, and a spirit of meekness?

text.NRSV: It is actually reported that there is sexual immorality among you, and of a kind that is not found even among Gentiles; for a man is living with his father’s wife.
text.Rotherham: On all hands, there is reported to be among you—fornication, and such fornication as this—which, not among the nations, [is found],—as that one should have, his father’s wife:—

text.NRSV: And you are arrogant! Should you not rather have mourned, so that he who has done this would have been removed from among you?
text.Rotherham: And, ye, have become puffed up, and have not rather mourned, in order that he might be removed out of your midst, who, this deed, hath wrought.

text.NRSV: For though absent in body, I am present in spirit; and as if present I have already pronounced judgment
text.Rotherham: For, I, indeed,—being absent in the body, but present in the spirit, have already judged, as present, him who, thus, this thing hath perpetrated:—

text.NRSV: in the name of the Lord Jesus on the man who has done such a thing. When you are assembled, and my spirit is present with the power of our Lord Jesus,
text.Rotherham: In the name of our Lord Jesus, ye being gathered together, and my spirit, with the power of our Lord Jesus,

text.NRSV: you are to hand this man over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that his spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord.
text.Rotherham: To deliver such a one as this, unto Satan, for the destruction of the flesh,—that, the spirit, may be saved in the day of the Lord.

text.NRSV: Your boasting is not a good thing. Do you not know that a little yeast leavens the whole batch of dough?
text.Rotherham: Unseemly, is your boast! Know ye not that, a little leaven, doth leaven, the whole of the lump?

text.NRSV: Clean out the old yeast so that you may be a new batch, as you really are unleavened. For our paschal lamb, Christ, has been sacrificed.
text.Rotherham: Purge ye out the old leaven, that ye may be a new lump,—even as ye are unleavened; for, our passover, hath even been sacrificed—Christ:

text.NRSV: Therefore, let us celebrate the festival, not with the old yeast, the yeast of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.
text.Rotherham: So then, let us be keeping the feast, not with old leaven, nor with leaven of baseness and wickedness,—but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.

text.NRSV: I wrote to you in my letter not to associate with sexually immoral persons—
text.Rotherham: I wrote unto you in my letter—not to be mixing yourselves up with fornicators;—

text.NRSV: not at all meaning the immoral of this world, or the greedy and robbers, or idolaters, since you would then need to go out of the world.
text.Rotherham: Not at all, [meaning] the fornicators of this world, or the covetous and extortioners, or idolaters,—else had ye been obliged, in that case, to go out of the world!

text.NRSV: But now I am writing to you not to associate with anyone who bears the name of brother or sister who is sexually immoral or greedy, or is an idolater, reviler, drunkard, or robber. Do not even eat with such a one.
text.Rotherham: But, now, I have written unto you not to be mixing yourselves up,—if anyone named a brother, be a fornicator, or covetous, or an idolater, or a reviler, or a drunkard, or an extortioner, with such a one as this, not so much, as to be eating together,

text.NRSV: For what have I to do with judging outsiders? Is it not those who are inside whom you are to judge?
text.Rotherham: For what have I to do to be judging them who are without? Do, ye, not judge, them who are within,

text.NRSV: God will judge those outside. “Drive out the wicked person from among yourselves.”
text.Rotherham: Whereas, them who are without, God, judgeth? Remove ye the wicked man from among, yourselves.

text.NRSV: When any of you has a grievance against another, do you dare to take it to court before the unrighteous, instead of taking it before the saints?
text.Rotherham: Dare any of you, having, a matter against his brother, sue for judgment before the unrighteous, and not before the saints?

text.NRSV: Do you not know that the saints will judge the world? And if the world is to be judged by you, are you incompetent to try these smallest cases?
text.Rotherham: Or know ye not that, the saints, shall judge the world? And, if, by you, the world, is to be judged, unworthy are ye of the smallest judgment-seats?

text.NRSV: Do you not know that we are to judge angels—to say nothing of ordinary matters?
text.Rotherham: Know ye not that we shall judge, messengers? and not, then, matters of this life?

text.NRSV: If you have ordinary cases, then, do you appoint as judges those who have no standing in the church?
text.Rotherham: If, then, for matters of this, life ye have judgment-seats, them who are of no account in the assembly, these, are ye seating thereupon?

text.NRSV: I say this to your shame. Can it be that there is no one among you wise enough to decide between one believer and another,
text.Rotherham: With a view to shame you, am I speaking. So, is it possible that there is among you—not so much as one wise man, who shall be able to judge between his brethren,—

text.NRSV: but a believer goes to court against a believer—and before unbelievers at that?
text.Rotherham: But, brother with brother, sueth for judgment, and that before unbelievers?

text.NRSV: In fact, to have lawsuits at all with one another is already a defeat for you. Why not rather be wronged? Why not rather be defrauded?
text.Rotherham: Already, indeed, it is an utter defeat for you, that ye are having, law-suits, one with another. Wherefore are ye not rather taking wrong? Wherefore are ye not rather suffering yourselves to be defrauded?

text.NRSV: But you yourselves wrong and defraud—and believers at that.
text.Rotherham: Nay! but, ye, are doing wrong, and defrauding,—and that [your] brethren.

text.NRSV: Do you not know that wrongdoers will not inherit the kingdom of God? Do not be deceived! Fornicators, idolaters, adulterers, male prostitutes, sodomites,
text.Rotherham: Or know ye not that, wrong-doers, shall not inherit, God’s kingdom? Be not deceiving yourselves:—neither fornicators, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor effeminate, nor sodomites,

text.NRSV: thieves, the greedy, drunkards, revilers, robbers—none of these will inherit the kingdom of God.
text.Rotherham: Nor thieves, nor covetous, nor drunkards, nor revilers, nor extortioners—shall inherit, God’s kingdom.

text.NRSV: And this is what some of you used to be. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and in the Spirit of our God.
text.Rotherham: And, these things, were some of you; but ye bathed them away,—but ye were sanctified, but ye were justified, in the name of [our] Lord Jesus Christ, and in the Spirit of our God.

text.NRSV: “All things are lawful for me,” but not all things are beneficial. “All things are lawful for me,” but I will not be dominated by anything.
text.Rotherham: All things, unto me, are allowable, but, not all things, are profitable: all things, unto me, are allowable, but, I, will not be brought under authority by any.

text.NRSV: “Food is meant for the stomach and the stomach for food,” and God will destroy both one and the other. The body is meant not for fornication but for the Lord, and the Lord for the body.
text.Rotherham: Foods for the belly, and the belly for foods; but, God, will set aside, both it and them. The body, however, is not for fornication, but, for the Lord,—and, the Lord, for the body.

text.NRSV: And God raised the Lord and will also raise us by his power.
text.Rotherham: God, moreover, hath both raised up, the Lord, and will raise, us, up from among [the dead] through his power.

text.NRSV: Do you not know that your bodies are members of Christ? Should I therefore take the members of Christ and make them members of a prostitute? Never!
text.Rotherham: Know ye not that, your bodies, are, members of Christ? Shall I, then, take away the members of the Christ and make them members of a harlot? Far be it!

text.NRSV: Do you not know that whoever is united to a prostitute becomes one body with her? For it is said, “The two shall be one flesh.”
text.Rotherham: Or know ye not that, he that joineth himself unto the harlot, is, one body? For, saith he, the two, shall become, one flesh;

text.NRSV: But anyone united to the Lord becomes one spirit with him.
text.Rotherham: But, he that joineth himself unto the Lord, is, one Spirit.

text.NRSV: Shun fornication! Every other sin a person commits is outside the body; but the fornicator sins against the body itself.
text.Rotherham: Flee fornication! Every sin, whatsoever a man shall commit, is, outside his body, but, he that committeth fornication, bringeth sin into his own body.

text.NRSV: Or do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, which you have from God, and that you are not your own?
text.Rotherham: Or know ye not that, your body, is, a shrine of the Holy Spirit that is within you, which ye have from God? And ye are not your own;—

text.NRSV: For you were bought with a price; therefore glorify God in your body.
text.Rotherham: For ye have been bought with a price! Therefore glorify God in your body.

text.NRSV: Now concerning the matters about which you wrote: “It is well for a man not to touch a woman.”
text.Rotherham: Now, concerning the things whereof ye wrote, it were, good, for a man, not to touch, a woman;

text.NRSV: But because of cases of sexual immorality, each man should have his own wife and each woman her own husband.
text.Rotherham: But, on account of fornications, let, each man, have, his own wife, and, each woman, have, her own husband:

text.NRSV: The husband should give to his wife her conjugal rights, and likewise the wife to her husband.
text.Rotherham: Unto the wife, let the husband render what is her due, and, in like manner, the wife also, unto the husband,—

text.NRSV: For the wife does not have authority over her own body, but the husband does; likewise the husband does not have authority over his own body, but the wife does.
text.Rotherham: The wife, over her own body, hath not authority, but the husband, and, in like manner, the husband also, over his own body, hath not authority, but the wife.

text.NRSV: Do not deprive one another, except perhaps by agreement for a set time, to devote yourselves to prayer, and then come together again, so that Satan may not tempt you because of your lack of self-control.
text.Rotherham: Be not depriving one another—unless perhaps by consent for a season, that ye may have leisure for prayer, and, again, may be together,—lest Satan be tempting you by reason of your want of self-control.

text.NRSV: This I say by way of concession, not of command.
text.Rotherham: This, however I am saying, by way of concession, not of injunction;

text.NRSV: I wish that all were as I myself am. But each has a particular gift from God, one of one kind and one of another.
text.Rotherham: Besides, I desire all men to be, even as myself,—but, each one, hath his, personal, gift from God, one, after this manner, and, another, after that.

text.NRSV: To the unmarried and the widows I say that it is well for them to remain unmarried as I am.
text.Rotherham: But I say, to the unmarried, and to the widows, good, were it for them, that they should abide, even as I;

text.NRSV: But if they are not practicing self-control, they should marry. For it is better to marry than to be aflame with passion.
text.Rotherham: But, if they have not self-control, let them marry, for, better, is it, to marry than to burn.

text.NRSV: To the married I give this command—not I but the Lord—that the wife should not separate from her husband
text.Rotherham: To the married, however, I give charge—not, I, but the Lord,—that, a wife, from her husband, do not depart,—

text.NRSV: (but if she does separate, let her remain unmarried or else be reconciled to her husband), and that the husband should not divorce his wife.
text.Rotherham: But, if she should even depart, let her remain unmarried, or, to her husband, be reconciled; and let not, a husband, leave, his wife.

text.NRSV: To the rest I say—I and not the Lord—that if any believer has a wife who is an unbeliever, and she consents to live with him, he should not divorce her.
text.Rotherham: But, unto the rest, say, I—not the Lord,—if, any brother, hath, a wife that believeth not, and, she, is well pleased to dwell with him, let him not leave her;

text.NRSV: And if any woman has a husband who is an unbeliever, and he consents to live with her, she should not divorce him.
text.Rotherham: And, a woman who hath a husband that believeth not, and, he, is well pleased to dwell with her, let her not leave her husband;—

text.NRSV: For the unbelieving husband is made holy through his wife, and the unbelieving wife is made holy through her husband. Otherwise, your children would be unclean, but as it is, they are holy.
text.Rotherham: For the husband that believeth not is hallowed in the wife, and the wife that believeth not is hallowed in the brother: else were, your children, impure, but, now, are they, pure.

text.NRSV: But if the unbelieving partner separates, let it be so; in such a case the brother or sister is not bound. It is to peace that God has called you.
text.Rotherham: But, if, the unbelieving, departeth, let him depart: the brother or the sister hath not come into bondage, in such cases, but, in peace, hath God called us.

text.NRSV: Wife, for all you know, you might save your husband. Husband, for all you know, you might save your wife.
text.Rotherham: For how knowest thou, O woman, whether, thy husband, thou shalt save? Or how knowest thou, O man, whether, thy wife, thou shalt save?

text.NRSV: However that may be, let each of you lead the life that the Lord has assigned, to which God has called you. This is my rule in all the churches.
text.Rotherham: If not—as the Lord hath distributed—unto each one, as God hath called, each one, so, let him be walking;—and, so, in all the assemblies, I ordain.

text.NRSV: Was anyone at the time of his call already circumcised? Let him not seek to remove the marks of circumcision. Was anyone at the time of his call uncircumcised? Let him not seek circumcision.
text.Rotherham: After being circumcised, was any called? let him not become uncircumcised; in uncircumcision, hath any been called? let him not be circumcised:

text.NRSV: Circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing; but obeying the commandments of God is everything.
text.Rotherham: The circumcision, is, nothing, and, the uncircumcision, is, nothing,—but keeping the commandments of God.

text.NRSV: Let each of you remain in the condition in which you were called.
text.Rotherham: Each one, in the calling wherein he was called, in the same, let him abide:

text.NRSV: Were you a slave when called? Do not be concerned about it. Even if you can gain your freedom, make use of your present condition now more than ever.
text.Rotherham: A bond-servant, wast thou called? let it not cause thee concern; but, if thou canst even become, free, rather use it.

text.NRSV: For whoever was called in the Lord as a slave is a freed person belonging to the Lord, just as whoever was free when called is a slave of Christ.
text.Rotherham: For, he who in the Lord was called, being a bond-servant, is, a freed-man of the Lord: in like manner, he that was called being, a freeman, is Christ’s bond-servant:—

text.NRSV: You were bought with a price; do not become slaves of human beings.
text.Rotherham: With a price, have ye been bought,—do not become bond-servants of men:

text.NRSV: In whatever condition you were called, brothers and sisters, there remain with God.
text.Rotherham: Each one, wherein he was called, brethren, in the same, let him abide with God.

text.NRSV: Now concerning virgins, I have no command of the Lord, but I give my opinion as one who by the Lord’s mercy is trustworthy.
text.Rotherham: But, concerning them who are virgin, injunction of the Lord, have I none; yet, a judgment, do I give, as one who hath obtained mercy from the Lord to be, faithful:

text.NRSV: I think that, in view of the impending crisis, it is well for you to remain as you are.
text.Rotherham: I consider this, then, to be, good, in the circumstances, by reason of the existing distress,—that it is, good for a man, so, to be:

text.NRSV: Are you bound to a wife? Do not seek to be free. Are you free from a wife? Do not seek a wife.
text.Rotherham: Hast thou become bound to a wife? do not seek to be loosed; hast thou become loosed from a wife? do not seek a wife.

text.NRSV: But if you marry, you do not sin, and if a virgin marries, she does not sin. Yet those who marry will experience distress in this life, and I would spare you that.
text.Rotherham: If, however, thou shouldst even marry, thou hast not sinned; and, if one who is virgin should marry, that one hath not sinned;—but, tribulation of the flesh, shall, such, have:—howbeit, I, spare you.

text.NRSV: I mean, brothers and sisters, the appointed time has grown short; from now on, let even those who have wives be as though they had none,
text.Rotherham: But, this, I say—the opportunity is, contracted for what remaineth—in order that, they who have wives, may be, as though they had none,

text.NRSV: and those who mourn as though they were not mourning, and those who rejoice as though they were not rejoicing, and those who buy as though they had no possessions,
text.Rotherham: And, they who weep, as though they wept not, and, they who rejoice, as though they rejoiced not, and, they who buy, as though they possessed not,

text.NRSV: and those who deal with the world as though they had no dealings with it. For the present form of this world is passing away.
text.Rotherham: And, they who use the world, as though they used it not to the full,—for the fashion of this world passeth away;

text.NRSV: I want you to be free from anxieties. The unmarried man is anxious about the affairs of the Lord, how to please the Lord;
text.Rotherham: And I desire you to be, without anxiety:—the unmarried man, is anxious for the things of the Lord, how he may please the Lord;

text.NRSV: but the married man is anxious about the affairs of the world, how to please his wife,
text.Rotherham: But, he that hath married, is anxious for the things of the world, how may please his wife—

text.NRSV: and his interests are divided. And the unmarried woman and the virgin are anxious about the affairs of the Lord, so that they may be holy in body and spirit; but the married woman is anxious about the affairs of the world, how to please her husband.
text.Rotherham: And he is divided; and, the unmarried woman, or the virgin, is anxious for the things of the Lord, that she may be holy [both] in her body and in her spirit; but, she that hath married, is anxious for the things of the world, how she may please her husband.

text.NRSV: I say this for your own benefit, not to put any restraint upon you, but to promote good order and unhindered devotion to the Lord.
text.Rotherham: This, however, with a view to your own profit, am I saying,—not that, a snare, upon you, I may cast, but with a view to what is comely, and devoted unto the Lord, without distraction.

text.NRSV: If anyone thinks that he is not behaving properly toward his fiancée, if his passions are strong, and so it has to be, let him marry as he wishes; it is no sin. Let them marry.
text.Rotherham: If however anyone considereth it behaving unseemly towards his virginity, if he should be beyond the bloom of life,—and, thus, it ought to come about, what he chooseth, let him do,—he sinneth not: let them marry:

text.NRSV: But if someone stands firm in his resolve, being under no necessity but having his own desire under control, and has determined in his own mind to keep her as his fiancée, he will do well.
text.Rotherham: But, he that standeth in his heart, steadfast, having no necessity, but hath authority concerning his own will, and, this, hath determined in his own heart,—to preserve his own virginity, well shall he do.

text.NRSV: So then, he who marries his fiancée does well; and he who refrains from marriage will do better.
text.Rotherham: So that, he that giveth in marriage his own virginity, doeth, well; and, he that giveth it not, shall do, better.

text.NRSV: A wife is bound as long as her husband lives. But if the husband dies, she is free to marry anyone she wishes, only in the Lord.
text.Rotherham: A wife, is bound for as long a time as her husband is living; but, if the husband have fallen asleep, she is, free, to be married unto whom she pleaseth,—only, in the Lord;

text.NRSV: But in my judgment she is more blessed if she remains as she is. And I think that I too have the Spirit of God.
text.Rotherham: But, happier, is she, if, so, she remain,—in my judgment; for I think, I also, have the Spirit of God.

text.NRSV: Now concerning food sacrificed to idols: we know that “all of us possess knowledge.” Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up.
text.Rotherham: Now, concerning the idol-sacrifices, we are aware—because we, all, have knowledge,—knowledge, puffeth up, but, love, buildeth up;

text.NRSV: Anyone who claims to know something does not yet have the necessary knowledge;
text.Rotherham: If anyone thinketh that he knoweth anything, not yet knoweth he, as he must needs come to know,

text.NRSV: but anyone who loves God is known by him.
text.Rotherham: But, if anyone loveth God, the same, is known of him—

text.NRSV: Hence, as to the eating of food offered to idols, we know that “no idol in the world really exists,” and that “there is no God but one.”
text.Rotherham: Concerning, then, the eating of idol-sacrifices, we are aware—that an idol is, nothing, in the world, and that, none, is God save one.

text.NRSV: Indeed, even though there may be so-called gods in heaven or on earth—as in fact there are many gods and many lords—
text.Rotherham: For, if indeed there are so-called gods, whether in heaven or upon earth,—as indeed there are gods many and lords many,

text.NRSV: yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist.
text.Rotherham: [Yet], to us, there is one God the Father, of whom are all things, and, we, for him; and one Lord Jesus Christ, through whom are all things, and, we, through him.

text.NRSV: It is not everyone, however, who has this knowledge. Since some have become so accustomed to idols until now, they still think of the food they eat as food offered to an idol; and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.
text.Rotherham: Howbeit, not in all, is the knowledge; but, some, by their familiarity, until even now, with the idol, as an idol-sacrifice, eat it, and, their conscience, being, weak, is defiled.

text.NRSV: “Food will not bring us close to God.” We are no worse off if we do not eat, and no better off if we do.
text.Rotherham: Food, indeed, will not commend us unto God,—neither, if we eat not, do we lack, nor, if we eat, do we abound.

text.NRSV: But take care that this liberty of yours does not somehow become a stumbling block to the weak.
text.Rotherham: But be taking heed, lest, by any means, your right, itself, become, an occasion of stumbling, unto the weak;

text.NRSV: For if others see you, who possess knowledge, eating in the temple of an idol, might they not, since their conscience is weak, be encouraged to the point of eating food sacrificed to idols?
text.Rotherham: For, if anyone should see [thee] who hast knowledge, in an idol-temple, reclining, will not, his conscience, being, weak, be built up for the eating of the idol-sacrifices?

text.NRSV: So by your knowledge those weak believers for whom Christ died are destroyed.
text.Rotherham: In fact, he that is weak, is being destroyed, by thy knowledge—the brother for whose sake Christ died.

text.NRSV: But when you thus sin against members of your family, and wound their conscience when it is weak, you sin against Christ.
text.Rotherham: But, in thus sinning against the brethren am wounding their conscience, seeing it is weak, against Christ, are ye sinning.

text.NRSV: Therefore, if food causes my brother or sister to fall, I will never eat meat, so that I may not cause one of them to fall.
text.Rotherham: Therefore, if food is an occasion of stumbling unto my brother, in nowise will I eat flesh unto the age that abideth,—that, I may not occasion, my brother, to stumble.

text.NRSV: Am I not free? Am I not an apostle? Have I not seen Jesus our Lord? Are you not my work in the Lord?
text.Rotherham: Am not free? Am I not an apostle? Jesus our Lord, have I not seen? Are not, ye, my work, in the Lord?

text.NRSV: If I am not an apostle to others, at least I am to you; for you are the seal of my apostleship in the Lord.
text.Rotherham: If, unto others, I am not an apostle, certainly at least, unto you, I am; for, the seal of my apostleship, ye, are in the Lord.

text.NRSV: This is my defense to those who would examine me.
text.Rotherham: My defence, unto them who are examining me, is this:

text.NRSV: Do we not have the right to our food and drink?
text.Rotherham: Have we not a right to eat and drink?

text.NRSV: Do we not have the right to be accompanied by a believing wife, as do the other apostles and the brothers of the Lord and Cephas?
text.Rotherham: Have we not a right to take round, a sister wife,—as even the rest of the apostles, and the brethren of the Lord, and Cephas?

text.NRSV: Or is it only Barnabas and I who have no right to refrain from working for a living?
text.Rotherham: Or have, only I and Barnabas, not a right to forbear working?

text.NRSV: Who at any time pays the cost of his own military service? Who plants a vineyard and does not eat any of its fruit? Or who tends a flock and does not get any of its milk?
text.Rotherham: Who serveth as a soldier, at his own charges, at any time? Who planteth a vineyard,—and, the fruit thereof, doth not eat? [Or] who shepherdeth a flock,—and, of the milk of the flock, doth not eat?

text.NRSV: Do I say this on human authority? Does not the law also say the same?
text.Rotherham: Is it, after the manner of men, that these things I am saying? Or doth not, even the law, the same things, say?

text.NRSV: For it is written in the law of Moses, “You shall not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain.” Is it for oxen that God is concerned?
text.Rotherham: For, in the law of Moses, it is written—Thou shalt not muzzle an ox when it is treading out the corn:—Is it, for the oxen, God is caring?

text.NRSV: Or does he not speak entirely for our sake? It was indeed written for our sake, for the plowman should plow in hope and the thresher thresh in hope of a share in the crop.
text.Rotherham: Or, for our sakes altogether, is he saying it? For our sakes, it was written; because, he that ploweth, ought, to plow, in hope,—and, he that thresheth, [to thresh] in hope of partaking.

text.NRSV: If we have sown spiritual good among you, is it too much if we reap your material benefits?
text.Rotherham: If, we, unto you, the things of the Spirit have sown, is it a great matter, if, we, of you, the things of the flesh shall reap?

text.NRSV: If others share this rightful claim on you, do not we still more? Nevertheless, we have not made use of this right, but we endure anything rather than put an obstacle in the way of the gospel of Christ.
text.Rotherham: If, others, of this right, over you, are partaking, [should] not rather, we? nevertheless, we used not this right; but, all [such] things, do we conceal,—lest we should cause any hindrance unto the glad message of the Christ.

text.NRSV: Do you not know that those who are employed in the temple service get their food from the temple, and those who serve at the altar share in what is sacrificed on the altar?
text.Rotherham: Know ye not that, they who labour in the holy rites, do eat the provisions out of the holy place? They who at the altar wait, do, with the altar, share?

text.NRSV: In the same way, the Lord commanded that those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel.
text.Rotherham: Thus, also, hath the Lord, appointed—that, they who the glad message tell, should, of the glad-message, live.

text.NRSV: But I have made no use of any of these rights, nor am I writing this so that they may be applied in my case. Indeed, I would rather die than that—no one will deprive me of my ground for boasting!
text.Rotherham: I, however, have not used any of these things; and have not written these things, in order that, so it should be done, in my case; for it were, good for me, rather to die than——my boast, shall, no man, make void!

text.NRSV: If I proclaim the gospel, this gives me no ground for boasting, for an obligation is laid on me, and woe to me if I do not proclaim the gospel!
text.Rotherham: For, if I be telling the glad-message, it is, with me, no matter of boasting; for, necessity, upon me, lieth,—for it is, Woe to me, if I should not be telling the glad-message;

text.NRSV: For if I do this of my own will, I have a reward; but if not of my own will, I am entrusted with a commission.
text.Rotherham: For, if, by choice, this thing I am doing, I have, a reward; but, if not by choice, with a stewardship, have I been entrusted!

text.NRSV: What then is my reward? Just this: that in my proclamation I may make the gospel free of charge, so as not to make full use of my rights in the gospel.
text.Rotherham: What, then, is my reward? that, in telling the glad-message, free of cost, I should put the glad-message,—to the end I should not use to the full my right in the glad-message.

text.NRSV: For though I am free with respect to all, I have made myself a slave to all, so that I might win more of them.
text.Rotherham: For, though free from all, unto all, myself, I enslaved,—that, the larger number, I might win:

text.NRSV: To the Jews I became as a Jew, in order to win Jews. To those under the law I became as one under the law (though I myself am not under the law) so that I might win those under the law.
text.Rotherham: Therefore became I, to the Jews, as, a Jew,—that, Jews, I might win; to them who were under law, as, under law, not being, myself, under law,—that, them who were under law, I might win;

text.NRSV: To those outside the law I became as one outside the law (though I am not free from God’s law but am under Christ’s law) so that I might win those outside the law.
text.Rotherham: To them who were without law, as, without law,—not being without law to God, but lawfully subject to Christ,—that I might win them who were without law.

text.NRSV: To the weak I became weak, so that I might win the weak. I have become all things to all people, that I might by all means save some.
text.Rotherham: I became, unto the weak, weak,—that, the weak, I might win;—to all men, have I become all things, that, by all means, some, I might save.

text.NRSV: I do it all for the sake of the gospel, so that I may share in its blessings.
text.Rotherham: But, all things, am I doing, for the sake of the glad-message, that a joint-partaker thereof, I may become.

text.NRSV: Do you not know that in a race the runners all compete, but only one receives the prize? Run in such a way that you may win it.
text.Rotherham: Know ye not that, they who, in a racecourse, run, all, indeed, run,—but, one, receiveth the prize? So, be running, that ye may lay hold.

text.NRSV: Athletes exercise self-control in all things; they do it to receive a perishable wreath, but we an imperishable one.
text.Rotherham: But, every man who striveth in the games, in all things, useth self-control;—they, indeed, then, that a corruptible crown, they may receive; but, we, an incorruptible!

text.NRSV: So I do not run aimlessly, nor do I box as though beating the air;
text.Rotherham: I, therefore, so, am running, as, not uncertainly, so, am boxing, as not thrashing air;

text.NRSV: but I punish my body and enslave it, so that after proclaiming to others I myself should not be disqualified.
text.Rotherham: But am beating my body under, and leading it captive, lest, by any means,—unto others, having proclaimed, [the contest], I myself, should be rejected.

text.NRSV: I do not want you to be unaware, brothers and sisters, that our ancestors were all under the cloud, and all passed through the sea,
text.Rotherham: For I wish not ye should be ignorant, brethren, that, all our fathers, were, under the cloud, and, all, passed through the sea,—

text.NRSV: and all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea,
text.Rotherham: And, all, immersed themselves into Moses, in the cloud, and in the sea;

text.NRSV: and all ate the same spiritual food,
text.Rotherham: And, all, did eat [the same] spiritual food,

text.NRSV: and all drank the same spiritual drink. For they drank from the spiritual rock that followed them, and the rock was Christ.
text.Rotherham: And, all, drank, the same spiritual drink,—for they continued to drink of the spiritual rock that followed them, and, the rock, was the Christ:—

text.NRSV: Nevertheless, God was not pleased with most of them, and they were struck down in the wilderness.
text.Rotherham: Nevertheless, with the most of them, God, was not well-pleased, for they were strewed along in the desert.

text.NRSV: Now these things occurred as examples for us, so that we might not desire evil as they did.
text.Rotherham: But, in these things, they became, ensamples for us, to the end we should not be covetous of evil things, even as, they, also coveted;

text.NRSV: Do not become idolaters as some of them did; as it is written, “The people sat down to eat and drink, and they rose up to play.”
text.Rotherham: Neither become ye, idolaters, as some of them,—as it is written—The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to be making sport;

text.NRSV: We must not indulge in sexual immorality as some of them did, and twenty-three thousand fell in a single day.
text.Rotherham: Neither let us commit fornication, as, some of them, committed fornication, and there fell, in one day, three and twenty thousand;

text.NRSV: We must not put Christ to the test, as some of them did, and were destroyed by serpents.
text.Rotherham: Neither let us be putting the Lord to the test, as, some of them, put him to the test, and, by the serpents, were perishing;

text.NRSV: And do not complain as some of them did, and were destroyed by the destroyer.
text.Rotherham: Neither be ye murmuring, even, as some of them, murmured, and perished by the destroyer.

text.NRSV: These things happened to them to serve as an example, and they were written down to instruct us, on whom the ends of the ages have come.
text.Rotherham: But, these things, by way of type, were happening unto them, and were written with a view to our admonition, unto whom, the ends of the ages, have reached along.

text.NRSV: So if you think you are standing, watch out that you do not fall.
text.Rotherham: So that, he that thinketh he is standing, let him take heed lest he fall.

text.NRSV: No testing has overtaken you that is not common to everyone. God is faithful, and he will not let you be tested beyond your strength, but with the testing he will also provide the way out so that you may be able to endure it.
text.Rotherham: Trial, hath not taken you, save such as man can bear; faithful, moreover is God, who will not suffer you to be tried above what ye are able, but will make, with the trial, also the way of escape, that ye may be able to hold out.

text.NRSV: Therefore, my dear friends, flee from the worship of idols.
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, my beloved, flee from idolatry,—

text.NRSV: I speak as to sensible people; judge for yourselves what I say.
text.Rotherham: As, to prudent men, I speak,—judge, ye, what I say:—

text.NRSV: The cup of blessing that we bless, is it not a sharing in the blood of Christ? The bread that we break, is it not a sharing in the body of Christ?
text.Rotherham: The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not, a sharing together of the blood of the Christ? The loaf which we break, is it not, a sharing together of the body of the Christ?

text.NRSV: Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body, for we all partake of the one bread.
text.Rotherham: Because, one loaf, one body, we, the many, are, for we, all, of the one loaf, partake.

text.NRSV: Consider the people of Israel; are not those who eat the sacrifices partners in the altar?
text.Rotherham: Be looking at Israel after the flesh:—are not, they who eat the sacrifices, joint partakers with the altar?

text.NRSV: What do I imply then? That food sacrificed to idols is anything, or that an idol is anything?
text.Rotherham: What, then, am I saying?—that, an idol-sacrifice, is anything? or that, an idol, is anything?

text.NRSV: No, I imply that what pagans sacrifice, they sacrifice to demons and not to God. I do not want you to be partners with demons.
text.Rotherham: On the contrary—that, the things which the nations sacrifice, unto demons, and not unto God, they sacrifice; and I wish not that ye should become, sharers together with the demons!

text.NRSV: You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons. You cannot partake of the table of the Lord and the table of demons.
text.Rotherham: Ye cannot be drinking—a cup of the Lord, and a cup of demons; ye cannot be partaking of, a table of the Lord, and a table of demons.

text.NRSV: Or are we provoking the Lord to jealousy? Are we stronger than he?
text.Rotherham: Or are we to provoke the Lord to jealousy? Are we mightier than he?

text.NRSV: “All things are lawful,” but not all things are beneficial. “All things are lawful,” but not all things build up.
text.Rotherham: All things are allowable—but, not all things, are profitable,—all things are allowable—but, not all things, upbuild.

text.NRSV: Do not seek your own advantage, but that of others.
text.Rotherham: Let, no one, be seeking, what concerneth himself, but that which concerneth some one else.

text.NRSV: Eat whatever is sold in the meat market without raising any question on the ground of conscience,
text.Rotherham: Whatsoever, in the market, is sold, eat,—asking no question, for conscience sake;

text.NRSV: for “the earth and its fullness are the Lord’s.”
text.Rotherham: For, unto the Lord, belongeth the earth, and the fullness thereof.

text.NRSV: If an unbeliever invites you to a meal and you are disposed to go, eat whatever is set before you without raising any question on the ground of conscience.
text.Rotherham: If one of them who believe not invite you, and ye are disposed to go, whatsoever is set before you, eat,—asking no question, for conscience sake.

text.NRSV: But if someone says to you, “This has been offered in sacrifice,” then do not eat it, out of consideration for the one who informed you, and for the sake of conscience—
text.Rotherham: But, if anyone should say unto you,—This, is, a holy sacrifice, do not eat, for that man’s sake who disclosed it, and for conscience sake:—

text.NRSV: I mean the other’s conscience, not your own. For why should my liberty be subject to the judgment of someone else’s conscience?
text.Rotherham: But, conscience, I mean, not thine own, but the other’s,—for why is my freedom to be judged by another’s conscience?

text.NRSV: If I partake with thankfulness, why should I be reviled because of something for which I give thanks?
text.Rotherham: If, I, with gratitude, partake, why am I to be defamed, as to that for which, I, give thanks?

text.NRSV: So, whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do everything for the glory of God.
text.Rotherham: Whether, therefore, ye eat or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do, all things to the glory of God.

text.NRSV: Give no offense to Jews or to Greeks or to the church of God,
text.Rotherham: Give no occasion of stumbling—whether to Jews, or to Greeks, or to the assembly of God:

text.NRSV: just as I try to please everyone in everything I do, not seeking my own advantage, but that of many, so that they may be saved.
text.Rotherham: Even as, I also, in all things do please all, not seeking my own profit, but that of the many, that they may be saved.

text.NRSV: Be imitators of me, as I am of Christ.
text.Rotherham: Become imitators of me,—even as, I also, [am] of Christ.

text.NRSV: I commend you because you remember me in everything and maintain the traditions just as I handed them on to you.
text.Rotherham: Now I praise you, that, in all things, ye have me in remembrance, and, even as I delivered unto you the instructions, ye hold them fast.

text.NRSV: But I want you to understand that Christ is the head of every man, and the husband is the head of his wife, and God is the head of Christ.
text.Rotherham: But I wish you to know—that, the head of every man, is, the Christ, and, the head of a woman, is, the man; and, the head of the Christ, is, God.

text.NRSV: Any man who prays or prophesies with something on his head disgraces his head,
text.Rotherham: Every man, praying, or prophesying, having anything upon his head, putteth to shame his head;

text.NRSV: but any woman who prays or prophesies with her head unveiled disgraces her head—it is one and the same thing as having her head shaved.
text.Rotherham: But, every woman, praying, or prophesying, with her head, unveiled, putteth to shame her head,—for it is, one and the same, with her having been shaven.

text.NRSV: For if a woman will not veil herself, then she should cut off her hair; but if it is disgraceful for a woman to have her hair cut off or she should be shaved, she should wear a veil.
text.Rotherham: For, if a woman doth not veil herself, let her also be shorn; but, if it were a shame in a woman to be shorn or shaven, let her be veiled.

text.NRSV: For a man ought not to have his head veiled, since he is the image and reflection of God; but woman is the reflection of man.
text.Rotherham: For, a man, indeed, ought not to be veiling his head, being, the image and glory of God,—but, the woman, is the glory of man;

text.NRSV: Indeed, man was not made from woman, but woman from man.
text.Rotherham: For, man, is not, of woman, but, woman, of man;

text.NRSV: Neither was man created for the sake of woman, but woman for the sake of man.
text.Rotherham: For also man was not created, for the sake of the woman, but woman, for the sake of the man.

text.NRSV: For this reason a woman ought to have a symbol of authority on her head, because of the angels.
text.Rotherham: For this cause, ought the woman to have, permission, upon her head, because of the messengers.

text.NRSV: Nevertheless, in the Lord woman is not independent of man or man independent of woman.
text.Rotherham: Howbeit, neither is woman apart from man, nor man apart from woman—in the Lord;

text.NRSV: For just as woman came from man, so man comes through woman; but all things come from God.
text.Rotherham: For, just as the woman is of the man, so, also the man, is through means of the woman;—but, all things, are of God.

text.NRSV: Judge for yourselves: is it proper for a woman to pray to God with her head unveiled?
text.Rotherham: Among your own selves, judge ye,—Is it becoming for a woman,—unveiled, to be praying unto God?

text.NRSV: Does not nature itself teach you that if a man wears his hair long, it is degrading to him,
text.Rotherham: Doth not, even nature herself, teach you—that, if, a man, have long hair, it is a dishonour to him;

text.NRSV: but if a woman has long hair, it is her glory? For her hair is given to her for a covering.
text.Rotherham: But, if, a woman, have long hair, it is a glory to her, for, her long hair, instead of a veil, hath been given to her.

text.NRSV: If anyone is disposed to be contentious—we have no such custom, nor do the churches of God.
text.Rotherham: But, if anyone thinketh to be contentious, we, have no such custom,—nor yet the assemblies of God.

text.NRSV: Now in the following instructions I do not commend you, because when you come together it is not for the better but for the worse.
text.Rotherham: But, in giving you the following charge, I praise you not,—in that, not for the better, but for the worse, ye come together.

text.NRSV: For, to begin with, when you come together as a church, I hear that there are divisions among you; and to some extent I believe it.
text.Rotherham: For, first of all,—when ye come together in assembly, I hear that divisions among you exist,—and, in part, I believe it;

text.NRSV: Indeed, there have to be factions among you, for only so will it become clear who among you are genuine.
text.Rotherham: For there must needs be, even parties among you, that, the approved, [themselves] may become manifest among you.

text.NRSV: When you come together, it is not really to eat the Lord’s supper.
text.Rotherham: When, therefore, ye come together into one place, it is not to eat, a supper unto the Lord;

text.NRSV: For when the time comes to eat, each of you goes ahead with your own supper, and one goes hungry and another becomes drunk.
text.Rotherham: For, each one, taketh beforehand, his own supper, in your eating, and, one, indeed, is hungry, whereas, another, is drinking to excess.

text.NRSV: What! Do you not have homes to eat and drink in? Or do you show contempt for the church of God and humiliate those who have nothing? What should I say to you? Should I commend you? In this matter I do not commend you!
text.Rotherham: What! have ye not houses for your eating and drinking? or, the assembly of God, do ye despise, and, put to shame them who have nothing? What am I to say to you? Shall I praise you? In this, I praise you not.

text.NRSV: For I received from the Lord what I also handed on to you, that the Lord Jesus on the night when he was betrayed took a loaf of bread,
text.Rotherham: For, I myself, received from the Lord—that which I also delivered unto you,—how that, the Lord Jesus, in the night in which he was being delivered up, took a loaf,

text.NRSV: and when he had given thanks, he broke it and said, “This is my body that is for you. Do this in remembrance of me.”
text.Rotherham: And, giving thanks, brake it, and said—This, is my body, which is for you,—this, do ye, in remembrance of me;

text.NRSV: In the same way he took the cup also, after supper, saying, “This cup is the new covenant in my blood. Do this, as often as you drink it, in remembrance of me.”
text.Rotherham: In the same manner, the cup also, after they had taken supper, saying—This cup, is, the new covenant in my blood, this, do ye, as often soever as ye may be drinking it, in remembrance of me.

text.NRSV: For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes.
text.Rotherham: For, as often as ye may be eating this loaf, and, the cup, may be drinking, The death of the Lord, do ye announce, until he come.

text.NRSV: Whoever, therefore, eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be answerable for the body and blood of the Lord.
text.Rotherham: So that, whosoever may be eating the loaf, or drinking the cup of the Lord, in an unworthy manner, shall be responsible for the body and blood of the Lord.

text.NRSV: Examine yourselves, and only then eat of the bread and drink of the cup.
text.Rotherham: But let a man be proving himself,—and, so, of the loaf, let him eat, and, of the cup, let him drink;

text.NRSV: For all who eat and drink without discerning the body, eat and drink judgment against themselves.
text.Rotherham: For, he that is eating and drinking,—judgment unto himself, doth eat and drink,—if he be not setting apart the body.

text.NRSV: For this reason many of you are weak and ill, and some have died.
text.Rotherham: For this cause, many among you are weak and sickly; and, not a few, are falling asleep.

text.NRSV: But if we judged ourselves, we would not be judged.
text.Rotherham: If, however, we had been setting, ourselves, apart, we had not, in that case, been coming under judgment;

text.NRSV: But when we are judged by the Lord, we are disciplined so that we may not be condemned along with the world.
text.Rotherham: But, being brought under judgment, by the Lord, are we being disciplined, lest, with the world, we should be condemned.

text.NRSV: So then, my brothers and sisters, when you come together to eat, wait for one another.
text.Rotherham: So then, my brethren,—when ye come together for the eating, unto one another, be giving welcome:—

text.NRSV: If you are hungry, eat at home, so that when you come together, it will not be for your condemnation. About the other things I will give instructions when I come.
text.Rotherham: If anyone be hungry, at home, let him eat, lest, unto judgment, ye be coming together. And, the remaining things, whensoever I come, I will set in order.

text.NRSV: Now concerning spiritual gifts, brothers and sisters, I do not want you to be uninformed.
text.Rotherham: But, concerning the spiritually gifted, brethren, I am not wishing you to be ignorant.

text.NRSV: You know that when you were pagans, you were enticed and led astray to idols that could not speak.
text.Rotherham: Ye know that, when ye were of the nations, unto the dumb idols, howsoever ye were being led, ye were seduced.

text.NRSV: Therefore I want you to understand that no one speaking by the Spirit of God ever says “Let Jesus be cursed!” and no one can say “Jesus is Lord” except by the Holy Spirit.
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, I give you to know—that, no one, in God’s Spirit speaking, saith—Accursed Jesus! and, no one, can say—Lord Jesus! save in [the] Holy Spirit.

text.NRSV: Now there are varieties of gifts, but the same Spirit;
text.Rotherham: But, distributions of gifts, there are, yet the same Spirit,

text.NRSV: and there are varieties of services, but the same Lord;
text.Rotherham: And, distributions of ministries, there are, and the same Lord,

text.NRSV: and there are varieties of activities, but it is the same God who activates all of them in everyone.
text.Rotherham: And, distributions of energies, there are, and the same God—who energiseth all things in all.

text.NRSV: To each is given the manifestation of the Spirit for the common good.
text.Rotherham: But, unto each one, is given the manifesting of the Spirit, with a view to that which is profitable;

text.NRSV: To one is given through the Spirit the utterance of wisdom, and to another the utterance of knowledge according to the same Spirit,
text.Rotherham: For, unto one, indeed, through the Spirit, is given, a word of wisdom, but, unto another, a word of knowledge, according to the same Spirit;—

text.NRSV: to another faith by the same Spirit, to another gifts of healing by the one Spirit,
text.Rotherham: Unto a different one, faith, in the same Spirit, and, unto another, gifts of healings, in the one Spirit,

text.NRSV: to another the working of miracles, to another prophecy, to another the discernment of spirits, to another various kinds of tongues, to another the interpretation of tongues.
text.Rotherham: And, unto another, energies of mighty works, [and], unto another, prophesying, [and], unto another, discriminations of spirits,—unto a different one, kinds of tongues, and, unto another, translation of tongues;—

text.NRSV: All these are activated by one and the same Spirit, who allots to each one individually just as the Spirit chooses.
text.Rotherham: But, all these, energiseth the one and the same Spirit, distributing unto each one, peculiarly, even as it is disposed.

text.NRSV: For just as the body is one and has many members, and all the members of the body, though many, are one body, so it is with Christ.
text.Rotherham: For, just as, the body, is one, and yet hath many members, but, all the members of the body, though many, are one body, so, also, the Christ;—

text.NRSV: For in the one Spirit we were all baptized into one body—Jews or Greeks, slaves or free—and we were all made to drink of one Spirit.
text.Rotherham: For, even to one Spirit, we all, into one body, have been immersed,—whether Jews or Greeks, whether bond or free,—and, all, of one Spirit, have been caused to drink.

text.NRSV: Indeed, the body does not consist of one member but of many.
text.Rotherham: For, the body also, is not one member, but many:

text.NRSV: If the foot would say, “Because I am not a hand, I do not belong to the body,” that would not make it any less a part of the body.
text.Rotherham: Though the foot should say—Because I am not a hand, I am not of the body, Is it, therefore, not, of the body?

text.NRSV: And if the ear would say, “Because I am not an eye, I do not belong to the body,” that would not make it any less a part of the body.
text.Rotherham: And, though the ear should say—Because I am not an eye, I am not of the body, Is it, therefore, not, of the body?

text.NRSV: If the whole body were an eye, where would the hearing be? If the whole body were hearing, where would the sense of smell be?
text.Rotherham: If, the whole body, were, an eye, where were the hearing? If, the whole were, hearing, where were, the smelling?

text.NRSV: But as it is, God arranged the members in the body, each one of them, as he chose.
text.Rotherham: But, now, hath God set the members, each one of them in the body, even as he pleased.

text.NRSV: If all were a single member, where would the body be?
text.Rotherham: But, if they all had been one member, Where had been, the body?

text.NRSV: As it is, there are many members, yet one body.
text.Rotherham: Now, however there are many members, yet one body;

text.NRSV: The eye cannot say to the hand, “I have no need of you,” nor again the head to the feet, “I have no need of you.”
text.Rotherham: [And] the eye cannot say unto the hand—I have no need of thee,—nor, again, the head, unto the feet—I have no need of you:—

text.NRSV: On the contrary, the members of the body that seem to be weaker are indispensable,
text.Rotherham: Nay! much more, the members which seem to be weaker are necessary,

text.NRSV: and those members of the body that we think less honorable we clothe with greater honor, and our less respectable members are treated with greater respect;
text.Rotherham: And, the parts of the body which we deem to be less honourable, on these, more abundant honour, do we bestow, and, our uncomely parts, have, more abundant comeliness,

text.NRSV: whereas our more respectable members do not need this. But God has so constructed the body, giving the greater honor to the inferior member,
text.Rotherham: Whereas, our comely parts, have, no need. But, God, hath tempered the body together,—unto that which was lacking, giving more abundant honour;—

text.NRSV: so that there may be no dissension in the body, but the members may have the same care for one another.
text.Rotherham: That there might be no division in the body, but that the members might have, the same, care, one for another;

text.NRSV: If one member suffers, all suffer together with it; if one member is honored, all rejoice together with it.
text.Rotherham: And, whether one member, suffereth, all the members, suffer with it, or, a member is glorified, all the members, rejoice together.

text.NRSV: Now you are the body of Christ and individually members of it.
text.Rotherham: Now, ye, are the body of Christ, and members severally;—
